status.state_description: Waiting for human approval of the plan
status.product_description: Enterprise identity and access management (IAM) with SSO (SAML, OIDC), MFA, RBAC, SCIM provisioning, audit logging, and compliance reporting.

plan.description: Define business requirements, user personas, and process flows for the enterprise IAM platform covering SSO, MFA, RBAC, SCIM, audit logging, and compliance reporting.
acceptance_criteria: BRD covers all features: SSO (SAML, OIDC), MFA, RBAC, SCIM, audit logging, compliance reporting
At least 20 user stories with acceptance criteria defined
Process flows documented for onboarding, de-provisioning, access review, and incident response
ROI analysis with measurable success metrics (time-to-provision, audit pass rate)
Integration points with existing systems mapped
Migration plan drafted

plan.description: Produce the product requirements document (PRD), feature roadmap, and prioritization framework for the identity_platform solution.
acceptance_criteria: PRD reviewed and signed off by business analyst and security stakeholders
Roadmap defines MVP scope, phase gates, and release criteria
Success metrics include MTTD, provisioning latency, compliance audit pass rate
Prioritization matrix maps features to business impact and compliance requirements

plan.description: Draft terms of service, privacy policy, data processing agreements (DPA), and IP review for the identity_platform. Assess GDPR, CCPA, and HIPAA applicability.
acceptance_criteria: ToS and privacy policy reviewed by legal counsel
DPA template covers sub-processor obligations and data residency
IP review confirms no third-party license conflicts in chosen OSS dependencies
Licensing strategy documented for enterprise deployment scenarios

plan.description: Perform threat modeling (STRIDE), produce a threat model document, define security controls, and create a penetration test plan for the IAM platform.
acceptance_criteria: STRIDE analysis completed for all trust boundaries (IdP, SP, SCIM endpoint, admin API)
Security controls mapped to ISO 27001 Annex A and SOC 2 CC series
Penetration test plan covers OWASP Top 10 and OAuth/OIDC-specific attack vectors
SBOM generated listing all runtime dependencies with known CVE status

plan.description: Design user flows, wireframes, and interaction specs for the admin console (user management, policy editor, audit viewer) and end-user login/MFA enrollment experiences.
acceptance_criteria: Wireframes produced for all listed screens at mid-fidelity
Accessibility audit confirms WCAG 2.1 AA compliance targets
Admin console UX validated with at least 3 IT admin personas via usability review
Design tokens exported for frontend implementation

plan.description: Design OpenAPI specs for all platform APIs: authentication (OIDC/SAML), SCIM 2.0 provisioning, RBAC management, MFA enrollment, audit log retrieval, and compliance report generation.
acceptance_criteria: OpenAPI 3.1 spec validates with no errors via swagger-parser
SCIM 2.0 endpoints conform to RFC 7643 and RFC 7644
OIDC discovery document includes all required metadata fields per OpenID Connect spec
All endpoints include authentication schemes, request/response schemas, and error codes

plan.description: Design and implement the database schema for users, groups, roles, permissions, SCIM attributes, MFA devices, sessions, audit events, and compliance report metadata.
acceptance_criteria: Schema normalized to 3NF with documented denormalization exceptions
audit_events table partitioned by month to support 5-year retention
PII fields (email, phone) encrypted at rest using pgcrypto or application-level AES-256
All migrations run cleanly on fresh PostgreSQL 15 instance
Row-level security policies enforce tenant isolation

plan.description: Set up the monorepo structure, development toolchain, environment configuration, and agentic AI pattern (coordinator) for the identity_platform solution.

pattern_components: coordinator agent routes IAM requests to specialist agents

acceptance_criteria: Monorepo initialized with consistent linting (ruff, eslint) and pre-commit hooks
Environment config uses .env.example with all required variables documented
Docker Compose brings up all services with health checks passing
Coordinator agent pattern wired: routes to RBAC, audit, compliance specialist agents
Secret management configured via environment variables (no secrets in code)

plan.description: Implement the core authentication service: OIDC authorization server (authorization code flow, PKCE, token introspection, refresh), JWT issuance/validation, and session management.
acceptance_criteria: OIDC authorization code flow with PKCE passes OpenID Connect conformance tests
Tokens signed with RS256; JWK endpoint serves public keys
Refresh token rotation implemented with family invalidation on reuse detection
Sessions stored in Redis with configurable TTL and sliding expiry
Token introspection endpoint returns correct active/inactive status

plan.description: Implement SAML 2.0 Identity Provider (IdP) support: metadata generation, AuthnRequest handling, signed assertion issuance, and SP-initiated/IdP-initiated SSO flows.
acceptance_criteria: SAML AuthnResponse passes signature validation by at least 2 major SP test tools (SAMLtest.id)
Encrypted assertions supported with AES-256-CBC
Single Logout (SLO) cleans sessions across all registered SPs
Metadata endpoint returns valid SAML metadata with certificate and binding URLs
SP metadata import supports both URL-based and XML upload

plan.description: Implement Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA): TOTP enrollment/verification, SMS OTP via provider abstraction, WebAuthn/FIDO2 registration and assertion, and MFA policy enforcement per role.
acceptance_criteria: TOTP enrollment generates QR code and validates first code before marking enrolled
WebAuthn registration and assertion follow FIDO2 spec; tested with hardware key emulator
Backup codes generated as one-time use, hashed at rest
MFA policy enforces required factor per RBAC role group
Step-up authentication triggered for sensitive operations (role assignment, SCIM writes)

plan.description: Implement RBAC engine: role definition, permission assignment, hierarchical role inheritance, policy evaluation, and resource-level access control checks.

payload.model: hierarchical RBAC with attribute extensions (ABAC overlay)
payload.evaluation: OPA (Open Policy Agent) sidecar or native engine

acceptance_criteria: Role inheritance resolves correctly up to 5 levels deep without circular dependency
Policy evaluation returns allow/deny in under 5ms for 95th percentile
Permission sets support wildcard and negation patterns
Resource-scoped permissions enforce project/tenant boundaries
RBAC decisions logged to audit trail with full context

plan.description: Implement SCIM 2.0 provisioning service: User and Group CRUD, bulk operations, filter queries, ETag-based conflict detection, and webhook notifications for provisioning events.
acceptance_criteria: SCIM /Users and /Groups endpoints pass Okta SCIM test suite
Bulk operations handle up to 1000 operations per request
Filter queries support all SCIM filter operators (eq, ne, co, sw, gt, lt)
ETag conflict detection returns 412 Precondition Failed on stale updates
Provisioning events trigger webhooks with retry and delivery receipts

plan.description: Implement the audit logging service: structured event ingestion, tamper-evident chained log entries (hash chain), retention policy enforcement, and queryable event store.
acceptance_criteria: Every audit event includes: timestamp, actor, action, resource, outcome, IP, session_id, trace_id
Hash chain links each event to previous via SHA-256; chain integrity verifiable via GET /audit/verify
Retention policy enforces minimum 1-year hot storage, 5-year archival
Query API supports filter by actor, action, date range, resource with pagination
SIEM export supports CEF and JSON-LD formats

plan.description: Implement the compliance reporting service: evidence collection, control mapping (ISO 27001 / SOC 2), automated report generation, and scheduled report delivery.
acceptance_criteria: Control library covers all SOC 2 Trust Service Criteria (CC, A, C, PI, P series)
Evidence collector pulls data from audit, RBAC, and SCIM services automatically
Gap analysis report identifies controls with insufficient evidence with remediation guidance
PDF reports generated with executive summary, control status, and evidence appendix
Scheduled delivery sends reports via email on configurable cadence (weekly/monthly/quarterly)

plan.description: Implement the coordinator agentic pattern for the IAM platform: a central coordinator agent routes access review, anomaly detection, and compliance tasks to specialist agents (RBAC agent, audit agent, compliance agent), merges results, and surfaces proposals for human approval.

components.coordinator: Decomposes IAM tasks, routes to specialists, merges outputs
components.rbac_agent: Analyzes role assignments, detects over-provisioning, proposes cleanup
components.audit_agent: Detects anomalous auth patterns, surfaces HITL alerts
components.compliance_agent: Maps evidence to controls, flags gaps, drafts remediation proposals

payload.pattern_integration: SAGE proposal_store for HITL approval queue

acceptance_criteria: Coordinator successfully routes 'access review' task to rbac_agent and audit_agent in parallel
RBAC agent detects and proposes cleanup for roles with zero usage in 90 days
Audit agent flags >5 failed auth attempts from same IP within 1 hour as anomaly
Compliance agent generates SOC 2 gap report with no manual intervention
All agent proposals flow through HITL approval gate before execution

plan.description: Build the admin console React application: user directory, group management, RBAC policy editor, SCIM integration configuration, MFA policy settings, and session management views.
acceptance_criteria: All pages render without console errors on Chrome, Firefox, Safari
UserDirectory supports search, filter by group/role, and bulk operations
RBACPolicyEditor provides drag-and-drop role hierarchy visualization
SCIMIntegrations page shows provisioning sync status and last-sync timestamp
All forms include client-side validation matching API constraints

plan.description: Build the end-user login portal: branded login page, MFA enrollment wizard, WebAuthn registration, SSO redirect handling, and account recovery flow.
acceptance_criteria: Login page passes Lighthouse accessibility score >= 90
MFA enrollment wizard completes TOTP setup in under 3 user interactions
WebAuthn registration works on Chrome, Safari, and Firefox with platform authenticator
SAML SP-initiated SSO redirects complete within 2 seconds end-to-end
Account recovery flow enforces identity verification before credential reset

plan.description: Build the audit log viewer and compliance dashboard: filterable event timeline, integrity verification UI, compliance control status heatmap, and report download interface.
acceptance_criteria: Audit log viewer loads 1000 events without pagination lag (virtualized list)
Filter builder supports AND/OR combinations of all event fields
Integrity check indicator shows chain verification status per log segment
Compliance dashboard heatmap shows pass/fail/partial status per control
Report download triggers PDF generation and streams to browser

plan.description: Write unit and integration tests for all backend services: auth service (OIDC, SAML, MFA), RBAC engine, SCIM service, audit service, and compliance service.
acceptance_criteria: Line coverage >= 85% across all service modules
OIDC token flow tested end-to-end with real JWT validation
SAML assertion tests cover signed, encrypted, and malformed inputs
RBAC policy evaluation tests cover allow, deny, inheritance, and wildcard cases
SCIM filter query tests cover all RFC 7644 filter operators
Audit hash chain integrity test validates and detects tampering

plan.description: Produce a QA test plan, test case library, and execution report covering functional, security, and regression testing for the identity_platform.
acceptance_criteria: Test plan covers all PRD features with traceability matrix linking tests to requirements
Test case library contains >= 150 cases with expected outcomes
OWASP ZAP active scan shows zero high-severity findings on auth endpoints
Execution report signed off by QA engineer with pass/fail per test case
Regression suite runnable in CI in under 15 minutes

plan.description: Execute end-to-end system integration tests: SSO federation with 3 test SPs (Okta, Azure AD, Google), SCIM provisioning lifecycle, MFA step-up flows, and load testing at 10k concurrent sessions.
acceptance_criteria: SSO federation tests pass with Okta, Azure AD, and Google Workspace test tenants
SCIM lifecycle test creates, updates, deactivates, and reactivates 100 users without errors
MFA step-up triggers correctly on sensitive operations and cannot be bypassed
Load test sustains 10,000 concurrent sessions with p99 auth latency < 500ms
Audit chain integrity verified across 50,000 events in E2E test
Compliance report generated matches expected control statuses from test data
Load test simulates expected concurrent users
Failover scenario tested

plan.description: Produce compliance evidence artifacts for ISO 27001 and SOC 2 Type II: control mapping matrix, risk register, data flow diagrams, evidence collection, and audit readiness checklist.
acceptance_criteria: Control mapping covers all ISO 27001 Annex A controls with applicability justification
SOC 2 mapping covers all 5 Trust Service Categories (Security, Availability, Confidentiality, Processing Integrity, Privacy)
Risk register includes likelihood, impact, residual risk, and owner for each identified risk
Data flow diagrams show all PII flows with encryption status at each hop
Evidence package includes machine-readable artifacts linkable to specific controls
Audit readiness checklist reviewed by regulatory_specialist with no blocking gaps

plan.description: Set up Kubernetes deployment manifests, Helm charts, TLS termination, secrets management (Vault), and multi-region active-passive HA configuration for the identity_platform.

payload.secrets: HashiCorp Vault with Kubernetes auth method
payload.tls: cert-manager + Let's Encrypt or internal CA
payload.ha: multi-region active-passive with PostgreSQL streaming replication

acceptance_criteria: Helm charts deploy all services to Kubernetes with configurable resource limits
TLS terminates at ingress; all internal service comms use mTLS
Vault integration injects secrets as environment variables at pod startup
PostgreSQL failover completes in under 30 seconds with zero data loss (RPO=0, RTO<30s)
Horizontal pod autoscaler configured for auth-service with CPU and RPS triggers
All manifests pass kube-score security policy checks

plan.description: Build CI/CD pipelines, monitoring stack (Prometheus + Grafana), alerting rules, distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry), and SRE runbooks for the identity_platform.

payload.ci_cd: GitHub Actions with OIDC-based cloud auth (no long-lived keys)

acceptance_criteria: CI pipeline runs lint, unit tests, integration tests, and SAST on every PR
CD pipeline deploys to staging automatically; production requires manual approval gate
Grafana dashboards show auth latency, error rates, active sessions, SCIM sync status
All 5 alert rules fire correctly in a chaos test environment
OpenTelemetry traces span across auth-service, scim-service, and audit-service
SRE runbooks cover MTTR < 30 minutes for each P1 scenario

plan.description: Define SLA targets, operational runbooks, incident response procedures, capacity planning model, and on-call rotation structure for the identity_platform.
acceptance_criteria: SLA document approved by product manager and operations manager
Runbooks cover detection, triage, mitigation, and post-mortem steps for each scenario
Incident severity matrix maps symptoms to P1-P4 with escalation paths
Capacity model projects infrastructure costs at 10k, 100k, and 1M users
On-call rotation defined with pager integration (PagerDuty or Opsgenie)

plan.description: Produce user guides, IT admin onboarding documentation, developer integration guides (OIDC, SAML, SCIM), and compliance officer reporting tutorials for the identity_platform.
acceptance_criteria: End-user guide covers login, MFA enrollment (TOTP + WebAuthn), and account recovery with screenshots
Admin guide covers all console pages with step-by-step procedures
Developer guide includes working code samples in Python, Node.js, and Java for OIDC integration
Compliance officer guide walks through generating a SOC 2 evidence report end-to-end
All guides reviewed by a non-technical stakeholder for clarity
Admin guide separate from end-user guide
Role-based training paths defined

plan.description: Produce architecture documentation, API reference (auto-generated from OpenAPI spec), deployment guide, security hardening guide, and CHANGELOG for the identity_platform.

tools: Swagger UI / Redoc for API reference

acceptance_criteria: Architecture doc includes C4 context, container, and component diagrams
API reference generated from OpenAPI spec with no missing descriptions
Deployment guide covers Kubernetes install, Helm values, Vault setup, and smoke test
Security hardening guide covers TLS config, header policies, rate limiting, and secret rotation
CHANGELOG follows Keep a Changelog format with all MVP changes listed

flaws: GDPR right-to-erasure vs. immutable audit hash chain is an irreconcilable architectural conflict. Step 14 builds a SHA-256-chained immutable log; GDPR Article 17 requires deleting personal data on request. Erasing a record breaks the chain. No pseudonymization or selective-erasure strategy is defined anywhere in the plan.

Step 24 claims RPO=0 with PostgreSQL streaming replication. Async replication has inherent lag. True RPO=0 requires synchronous_commit=remote_write or 'on', which adds measurable write latency to every auth transaction. This tradeoff is never acknowledged or benchmarked.

Step 26 targets 99.99% availability (~52 min/year downtime) with active-passive multi-region and RTO<30s. Active-passive cannot hit 99.99% — a 30s failover alone consumes 60% of the annual budget in a single incident. Active-active with anycast DNS is required for that SLA, and it is absent from the infra design.

SAML IdP-initiated SSO (step 10) is explicitly included with no CSRF mitigation specified. This is a known critical attack vector (SAML Bearer Subject Confirmation bypasses RelayState binding). Including it without mandatory InResponseTo validation or deprecation guidance is a production security defect.

Session invalidation cascade on SCIM de-provisioning is never addressed. When Azure AD pushes a SCIM DELETE for a user (step 13), there is no explicit step that revokes active OIDC tokens, SAML sessions, and Redis session entries. The user is 'deleted' but can continue authenticating until token TTL expires.

Step 12 targets OPA sidecar p95 policy evaluation < 5ms. OPA over network sidecar introduces 1–3ms baseline per call. Under contention (SCIM bulk import triggering thousands of RBAC checks simultaneously), p95 routinely exceeds this. No benchmarking baseline or fallback (embedded OPA via go-rego) is specified.

Audit hash chain at enterprise event volume (step 14) is a sequential write bottleneck. A single hash chain across all event types means every high-frequency auth event (10k concurrent sessions from step 22) must write serially or the chain breaks. No sharding strategy, per-tenant sub-chains, or append-only log design is specified.

OAuth 2.0 token revocation (RFC 7009) is absent from both the API spec (step 6) and backend implementation (step 9). Token introspection is included, but without a POST /oauth2/revoke endpoint, clients cannot force-invalidate compromised access tokens — a compliance requirement under SOC 2 CC6.1.

SCIM bearer token provisioning model is undefined. Step 13 builds the SCIM server but no step defines how Okta/Azure AD/Google Workspace clients authenticate to it. SCIM clients use long-lived bearer tokens — their issuance, rotation, and scoping is never addressed, leaving a credential management gap.

Step 10 specifies AES-256-CBC for SAML encrypted assertions. AES-CBC is vulnerable to padding oracle attacks and is deprecated in the 2023 SAML V2.0 Security Considerations errata. AES-128-GCM key transport is the correct choice. Using a known-weak cipher in an identity system for regulated enterprise customers is unacceptable.
suggestions: Add a dedicated 'PII pseudonymization strategy' design step between steps 7 and 8. Define how audit events store user references (opaque IDs, not raw PII), and document the erasure procedure: delete the identity record, and the audit log retains the opaque ID — chain intact, PII gone.

Replace the RPO=0 claim in step 24 with an explicit synchronous replication policy decision: document the write latency penalty of synchronous_commit=on (~5-10ms per transaction on cross-AZ replication) and let the business decide RPO=0 vs. sub-second RPO.

Downgrade the SLA to 99.95% (~4.4 hours/year) to match active-passive architecture capabilities, or replace the infra design in step 24 with active-active multi-region using read replicas and a distributed session layer.

Add XML Signature Wrapping (XSW) attack vectors explicitly to the threat model in step 4, and require the SAML implementation (step 10) to use a library with proven XSW defenses (python3-saml's strict mode, not pysaml2 without hardened config). Add a dedicated XSW regression test in step 20.

Add step 13.1: 'SCIM de-provisioning event bus'. When SCIM DELETE fires, publish to an internal event (Redis pub/sub or Postgres LISTEN/NOTIFY) consumed by the auth service to invalidate all active sessions and tokens for that user_id synchronously.

Replace OPA network sidecar with embedded OPA (via python-rego or direct WASM evaluation) in the auth-service hot path. Reserve the sidecar for admin/policy management APIs only. This eliminates the network round-trip and makes 5ms p95 achievable.

Redesign the audit hash chain in step 14 to use per-tenant append-only chains with periodic anchor checkpointing (e.g., hourly Merkle root stored in a separate tamper-evident table). This enables parallel writes per tenant while maintaining verifiability.

Add a step between steps 8 and 9 for SCIM client credential management: define a /scim/credentials API for provisioning SCIM bearer tokens with scoped permissions, expiry, and rotation workflow. Wire rotation alerts into step 25's alerting rules.

Change SAML encrypted assertion cipher in step 10 to AES-128-GCM with RSA-OAEP key transport. Update the acceptance criteria to require validation against the OASIS SAML V2.0 Errata 05 (2023) security profile.

Add a credential compromise detection step (between steps 11 and 12): integrate k-anonymity HaveIBeenPwned API for password-based auth and define the alert-and-force-reenroll workflow for compromised credentials surfaced post-deployment.
missing: Account lockout and credential stuffing protection: no step defines lockout thresholds, CAPTCHA triggers, or IP-based rate limiting at the auth endpoint layer. OWASP ZAP in step 21 will find this gap but there is no implementation step to close it.

JWT algorithm confusion attack mitigation: no acceptance criterion in step 9 requires explicit algorithm pinning ('alg' header validation, rejection of 'none', prevention of RS256→HS256 downgrade). This is a P0 security control for any OIDC implementation.

Access certification / periodic access review workflow: step 16's coordinator agent mentions 'access review' as a use case, but no backend step implements the workflow (review campaign creation, reviewer assignment, approve/revoke decisions, audit trail). This is a core SOC 2 CC6.3 control.

LDAP/Active Directory bridge or directory sync connector: absent from all 28 steps. The majority of enterprise customers run on-prem AD. Without an LDAP connector or AD FS federation, the 'enterprise IAM' claim is significantly weakened for brownfield deployments.

Password policy engine: no step defines password complexity rules, breach password rejection, or history enforcement for password-based auth flows. NIST SP 800-63B compliance requires breach corpus checking.

Key rotation and JWK rollover procedure: step 9 creates RS256 signing keys but no operational procedure exists for zero-downtime key rotation. Enterprises require documented key lifecycle management for SOC 2 audit evidence.

Key generation ceremony documentation for ISO 27001: Annex A control A.10.1.2 (key management) requires documented key generation procedures, often with witnesses. Not present anywhere in the 28 steps.

Disaster recovery drill step: step 24 configures HA but no step validates the failover actually works end-to-end (DB failover, Redis failover, DNS TTL propagation, session continuity). A chaos engineering or DR drill step is absent.

PKCE state parameter and nonce validation specification: step 9 implements PKCE but no acceptance criterion explicitly requires state parameter CSRF protection or nonce replay prevention in the OIDC flow.

Rate limiting and DDoS protection at the API gateway layer: no WAF, request rate limiting (per-IP, per-client-id), or abuse detection is specified in any step. The auth endpoints are the highest-value targets in the system.
security_risks: XML Signature Wrapping (XSW): if pysaml2 is chosen without explicit strict-mode configuration, it is vulnerable to XSW attacks that allow an attacker to authenticate as any user by wrapping a valid signature around a forged assertion. Not in the threat model despite SAML being in scope.

IdP-initiated SSO CSRF: step 10 includes IdP-initiated SSO with no RelayState CSRF token requirement or InResponseTo binding enforcement. An attacker can craft a SAML response that authenticates a victim to a malicious SP session.

Refresh token family race condition: step 9 specifies 'family invalidation on reuse detection' but the implementation of a concurrent reuse window (two requests arrive within milliseconds of each other — one legitimate, one replayed) can cause false positives that lock out legitimate users or false negatives that allow attacker access.

SCIM injection: step 13 lists 'SCIM injection' as a focus area in the threat model (step 4) but no acceptance criterion in step 13 requires parameterized filter query parsing. SCIM filter expressions (RFC 7644 Section 3.4.2) parsed with string interpolation are injectable.

Audit log GDPR conflict enables compliance choice: the immutable hash chain in step 14 creates an architecture where the team must choose between GDPR compliance (erasure) and audit integrity (immutability). Without a design decision on pseudonymization, this will be discovered during the first GDPR erasure request in production, requiring an emergency architectural change.

Redis session store single point of failure: step 9 stores sessions in Redis with 'configurable TTL'. If Redis Cluster is not configured (step 8 mentions Redis but not Redis Cluster), a Redis failure terminates all active user sessions simultaneously — an auth outage indistinguishable from a P1 incident.

JWT 'alg: none' and algorithm substitution: without explicit algorithm pinning in the token validation middleware, a compromised or malicious client can submit JWTs with 'alg: none' or switch from RS256 to HS256 using the public key as the HMAC secret. Neither python-jose nor authlib protect against this by default without explicit configuration.

Secrets in Docker Compose for development: step 8 uses .env.example for environment variables in Docker Compose. Developers routinely copy .env.example to .env and commit it. No pre-commit hook to block .env commits is in the acceptance criteria, creating a credential leak vector in the development phase that can persist to production.
final_review.summary: This is a structurally sound IAM plan with impressive breadth — 28 steps, correct dependency ordering, appropriate technology choices, and genuine security-first thinking (STRIDE, SBOM, hash chain audit log, PKCE). However, it has three architectural defects that will hit production and cannot be patched post-launch: (1) the GDPR erasure vs. immutable audit chain conflict is a fundamental design contradiction requiring pseudonymization to be designed in from the database schema, not bolted on later; (2) the 99.99% SLA target is physically unachievable with active-passive failover and will become an SLA breach liability on the first major incident; (3) IdP-initiated SAML SSO and the absence of XSW attack mitigations represent critical authentication bypass risks in what is explicitly an identity security product. The plan also underspecifies operational concerns that will create SOC 2 audit failures: no access certification workflow, no key rotation procedure, no SCIM client credential lifecycle, and no LDAP bridge for the brownfield enterprise market this product targets. For an MVP targeting internal proof-of-concept with no compliance certification, score would be ~72. For the stated goal of production enterprise IAM with ISO 27001 and SOC 2 evidence collection, this requires resolving the three architectural conflicts and filling the seven missing controls before a production commitment.

flaws: GDPR right-to-erasure vs. immutable audit hash chain is an irreconcilable architectural conflict. Step 14 builds a SHA-256-chained immutable log; GDPR Article 17 requires deleting personal data on request. Erasing a record breaks the chain. No pseudonymization or selective-erasure strategy is defined anywhere in the plan.

Step 24 claims RPO=0 with PostgreSQL streaming replication. Async replication has inherent lag. True RPO=0 requires synchronous_commit=remote_write or 'on', which adds measurable write latency to every auth transaction. This tradeoff is never acknowledged or benchmarked.

Step 26 targets 99.99% availability (~52 min/year downtime) with active-passive multi-region and RTO<30s. Active-passive cannot hit 99.99% — a 30s failover alone consumes 60% of the annual budget in a single incident. Active-active with anycast DNS is required for that SLA, and it is absent from the infra design.

SAML IdP-initiated SSO (step 10) is explicitly included with no CSRF mitigation specified. This is a known critical attack vector (SAML Bearer Subject Confirmation bypasses RelayState binding). Including it without mandatory InResponseTo validation or deprecation guidance is a production security defect.

Session invalidation cascade on SCIM de-provisioning is never addressed. When Azure AD pushes a SCIM DELETE for a user (step 13), there is no explicit step that revokes active OIDC tokens, SAML sessions, and Redis session entries. The user is 'deleted' but can continue authenticating until token TTL expires.

Step 12 targets OPA sidecar p95 policy evaluation < 5ms. OPA over network sidecar introduces 1–3ms baseline per call. Under contention (SCIM bulk import triggering thousands of RBAC checks simultaneously), p95 routinely exceeds this. No benchmarking baseline or fallback (embedded OPA via go-rego) is specified.

Audit hash chain at enterprise event volume (step 14) is a sequential write bottleneck. A single hash chain across all event types means every high-frequency auth event (10k concurrent sessions from step 22) must write serially or the chain breaks. No sharding strategy, per-tenant sub-chains, or append-only log design is specified.

OAuth 2.0 token revocation (RFC 7009) is absent from both the API spec (step 6) and backend implementation (step 9). Token introspection is included, but without a POST /oauth2/revoke endpoint, clients cannot force-invalidate compromised access tokens — a compliance requirement under SOC 2 CC6.1.

SCIM clients use long-lived bearer tokens — their issuance, rotation, and scoping is never addressed, leaving a credential management gap.

Step 10 specifies AES-256-CBC for SAML encrypted assertions. AES-CBC is vulnerable to padding oracle attacks and is deprecated in the 2023 SAML V2.0 Security Considerations errata. AES-128-GCM key transport is the correct choice. Using a known-weak cipher in an identity system for regulated enterprise customers is unacceptable.

Replace the RPO=0 claim in step 24 with an explicit synchronous replication policy decision: document the write latency penalty of synchronous_commit=on (~5-10ms per transaction on cross-AZ replication) and let the business decide RPO=0 vs. sub-second RPO.

Downgrade the SLA to 99.95% (~4.4 hours/year) to match active-passive architecture capabilities, or replace the infra design in step 24 with active-active multi-region using read replicas and a distributed session layer.

Add XML Signature Wrapping (XSW) attack vectors explicitly to the threat model in step 4, and require the SAML implementation (step 10) to use a library with proven XSW defenses (python3-saml's strict mode, not pysaml2 without hardened config). Add a dedicated XSW regression test in step 20.

Add step 13.1: 'SCIM de-provisioning event bus'. When SCIM DELETE fires, publish to an internal event (Redis pub/sub or Postgres LISTEN/NOTIFY) consumed by the auth service to invalidate all active sessions and tokens for that user_id synchronously.

Replace OPA network sidecar with embedded OPA (via python-rego or direct WASM evaluation) in the auth-service hot path. Reserve the sidecar for admin/policy management APIs only. This eliminates the network round-trip and makes 5ms p95 achievable.

Redesign the audit hash chain in step 14 to use per-tenant append-only chains with periodic anchor checkpointing (e.g., hourly Merkle root stored in a separate tamper-evident table). This enables parallel writes per tenant while maintaining verifiability.

Add a step between steps 8 and 9 for SCIM client credential management: define a /scim/credentials API for provisioning SCIM bearer tokens with scoped permissions, expiry, and rotation workflow. Wire rotation alerts into step 25's alerting rules.

Change SAML encrypted assertion cipher in step 10 to AES-128-GCM with RSA-OAEP key transport. Update the acceptance criteria to require validation against the OASIS SAML V2.0 Errata 05 (2023) security profile.

Add a credential compromise detection step (between steps 11 and 12): integrate k-anonymity HaveIBeenPwned API for password-based auth and define the alert-and-force-reenroll workflow for compromised credentials surfaced post-deployment.
missing: Account lockout and credential stuffing protection: no step defines lockout thresholds, CAPTCHA triggers, or IP-based rate limiting at the auth endpoint layer. OWASP ZAP in step 21 will find this gap but there is no implementation step to close it.

JWT algorithm confusion attack mitigation: no acceptance criterion in step 9 requires explicit algorithm pinning ('alg' header validation, rejection of 'none', prevention of RS256→HS256 downgrade). This is a P0 security control for any OIDC implementation.

Access certification / periodic access review workflow: step 16's coordinator agent mentions 'access review' as a use case, but no backend step implements the workflow (review campaign creation, reviewer assignment, approve/revoke decisions, audit trail). This is a core SOC 2 CC6.3 control.

LDAP/Active Directory bridge or directory sync connector: absent from all 28 steps. The majority of enterprise customers run on-prem AD. Without an LDAP connector or AD FS federation, the 'enterprise IAM' claim is significantly weakened for brownfield deployments.

Password policy engine: no step defines password complexity rules, breach password rejection, or history enforcement for password-based auth flows. NIST SP 800-63B compliance requires breach corpus checking.

Key rotation and JWK rollover procedure: step 9 creates RS256 signing keys but no operational procedure exists for zero-downtime key rotation. Enterprises require documented key lifecycle management for SOC 2 audit evidence.

Key generation ceremony documentation for ISO 27001: Annex A control A.10.1.2 (key management) requires documented key generation procedures, often with witnesses. Not present anywhere in the 28 steps.

Disaster recovery drill step: step 24 configures HA but no step validates the failover actually works end-to-end (DB failover, Redis failover, DNS TTL propagation, session continuity). A chaos engineering or DR drill step is absent.

PKCE state parameter and nonce validation specification: step 9 implements PKCE but no acceptance criterion explicitly requires state parameter CSRF protection or nonce replay prevention in the OIDC flow.

Rate limiting and DDoS protection at the API gateway layer: no WAF, request rate limiting (per-IP, per-client-id), or abuse detection is specified in any step. The auth endpoints are the highest-value targets in the system.
security_risks: XML Signature Wrapping (XSW): if pysaml2 is chosen without explicit strict-mode configuration, it is vulnerable to XSW attacks that allow an attacker to authenticate as any user by wrapping a valid signature around a forged assertion. Not in the threat model despite SAML being in scope.

IdP-initiated SSO CSRF: step 10 includes IdP-initiated SSO with no RelayState CSRF token requirement or InResponseTo binding enforcement. An attacker can craft a SAML response that authenticates a victim to a malicious SP session.

Refresh token family race condition: step 9 specifies 'family invalidation on reuse detection' but the implementation of a concurrent reuse window (two requests arrive within milliseconds of each other — one legitimate, one replayed) can cause false positives that lock out legitimate users or false negatives that allow attacker access.

SCIM injection: step 13 lists 'SCIM injection' as a focus area in the threat model (step 4) but no acceptance criterion in step 13 requires parameterized filter query parsing. SCIM filter expressions (RFC 7644 Section 3.4.2) parsed with string interpolation are injectable.

Audit log GDPR conflict enables compliance choice: the immutable hash chain in step 14 creates an architecture where the team must choose between GDPR compliance (erasure) and audit integrity (immutability). Without a design decision on pseudonymization, this will be discovered during the first GDPR erasure request in production, requiring an emergency architectural change.

Redis session store single point of failure: step 9 stores sessions in Redis with 'configurable TTL'. If Redis Cluster is not configured (step 8 mentions Redis but not Redis Cluster), a Redis failure terminates all active user sessions simultaneously — an auth outage indistinguishable from a P1 incident.

JWT 'alg: none' and algorithm substitution: without explicit algorithm pinning in the token validation middleware, a compromised or malicious client can submit JWTs with 'alg: none' or switch from RS256 to HS256 using the public key as the HMAC secret. Neither python-jose nor authlib protect against this by default without explicit configuration.

Secrets in Docker Compose for development: step 8 uses .env.example for environment variables in Docker Compose. Developers routinely copy .env.example to .env and commit it. No pre-commit hook to block .env commits is in the acceptance criteria, creating a credential leak vector in the development phase that can persist to production.
history.summary: This is a structurally sound IAM plan with impressive breadth — 28 steps, correct dependency ordering, appropriate technology choices, and genuine security-first thinking (STRIDE, SBOM, hash chain audit log, PKCE). However, it has three architectural defects that will hit production and cannot be patched post-launch: (1) the GDPR erasure vs. immutable audit chain conflict is a fundamental design contradiction requiring pseudonymization to be designed in from the database schema, not bolted on later; (2) the 99.99% SLA target is physically unachievable with active-passive failover and will become an SLA breach liability on the first major incident; (3) IdP-initiated SAML SSO and the absence of XSW attack mitigations represent critical authentication bypass risks in what is explicitly an identity security product. The plan also underspecifies operational concerns that will create SOC 2 audit failures: no access certification workflow, no key rotation procedure, no SCIM client credential lifecycle, and no LDAP bridge for the brownfield enterprise market this product targets. For an MVP targeting internal proof-of-concept with no compliance certification, score would be ~72. For the stated goal of production enterprise IAM with ISO 27001 and SOC 2 evidence collection, this requires resolving the three architectural conflicts and filling the seven missing controls before a production commitment.
